cultural_lineage_roots: Fuses West African hunter tracking patterns with traditional Cameroonian dance openings.
movement_mechanics: Stand with feet hip-width apart, keeping your core tight and hands anchored at your chest center.
Take a large controlled step backward with your right leg, lowering your hips until your back knee is hovering just above the ground.
As you drop into the lunge, explosively extend both arms straight out to your sides, opening your chest wide to emulate a lion tracking prey.
Drive through your front heel to return to the starting standing position while snapping your hands back to your chest center.
Alternate legs on the next rep and repeat fluidly, maintaining continuous control.
audio_coaching_cues: Open the chest wide as you step back.
Keep your weight anchored firmly in your front heel.
Exhale sharply as you explode back to a standing position.

cultural_lineage_roots: Derived directly from the core stability conditioning drills of ancient Chinese martial arts.
movement_mechanics: Assume a strict high plank position, tracking your hands directly beneath your shoulders with your feet pressed close together.
Lower your entire torso toward the ground in a rigid line, keeping your elbows tucked tightly against your ribs to focus energy on the triceps.
Push through your palms to ascend, and as your arms reach full extension, explosively lift your right hand to tap your left shoulder.
Place your hand back down, lower into your next push-up rep, and tap your right shoulder with your left hand at the peak.
Keep your hips perfectly level throughout the entire set, resisting any side-to-side torso rocking.
audio_coaching_cues: Lock your core tight like a wooden board.
Keep your elbows tucked into your ribs; don't let them flare.
Stabilize your hips completely before you tap your shoulder.

target_muscle_group: Hip Flexors, Hamstrings, Spinal Erectors, Adductors
cultural_lineage_roots: Inspired by the deep-rooted grounding of the African baobab tree.
movement_mechanics: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width, toes pointing outward at 45 degrees.
Slowly lower hips straight down into a deep wide squat. Keep spine tall and chest lifted.
Place both hands on inner knees and gently press outward to open the hips wider.
Hold this deep position for 5 full breaths.
Rise back to standing. Raise arms overhead and stretch fingertips toward the sky.
Hold the overhead reach for 3 breaths, then lower arms and repeat.
audio_coaching_cues: Imagine your feet are roots sinking deep into the earth.
Keep your spine as tall and straight as a baobab trunk.
Breathe deeply into the stretch. Never force it.

target_muscle_group: Calves, Hip Abductors, Cardiovascular System, Coordination
cultural_lineage_roots: Modeled after the rhythmic lateral footwork patterns of Ghanaian Kente cloth weavers.
movement_mechanics: Stand with feet together, arms relaxed, core lightly engaged.
Step right foot out to the right, then cross left foot behind right ankle.
Step right foot out again, then bring left foot to meet it.
Reverse the pattern immediately going left.
Maintain a steady, rhythmic pace. Arms swing naturally.
audio_coaching_cues: Keep a steady rhythm like a drumbeat. Do not rush.
Stay light on the balls of your feet.
Swing your arms naturally with the movement.

target_muscle_group: Deltoids, Upper Trapezius, Triceps, Core Stabilizers
cultural_lineage_roots: Drawn from the daily strength rituals of riverside communities carrying water vessels overhead.
movement_mechanics: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a filled water container in each hand at shoulder height.
Brace core tightly and press both containers straight overhead until arms fully extended.
Hold the top position for 2 seconds, squeezing shoulder muscles.
Lower containers slowly back to shoulder height. Take 2 full seconds on descent.
Repeat without resting at the bottom. Maintain constant tension.
audio_coaching_cues: Press straight up. Do not let the containers drift forward.
Squeeze your core to protect your lower back.
Control the descent. Slow lowering builds more strength.

target_muscle_group: Triceps, Anterior Deltoids, Pectoralis Minor, Core
cultural_lineage_roots: Inspired by the ceremonial rising and sitting of traditional village chiefs on carved wooden thrones.
movement_mechanics: Sit on the edge of a sturdy chair. Place palms on the edge beside your hips.
Slide hips forward off the chair edge so weight is supported by your hands.
Lower body slowly by bending elbows until upper arms are roughly parallel to floor.
Press through palms to push back up. Squeeze triceps at the top.
Keep your back close to the chair throughout.
audio_coaching_cues: Lower yourself slowly. Count to 3 on the way down.
Keep elbows pointing straight back, not flaring out.
Sit tall at the top like a chief commanding respect.

target_muscle_group: Gastrocnemius, Soleus, Ankle Stabilizers, Achilles Complex
cultural_lineage_roots: Derived from the natural calf development of Mount Cameroon trekkers.
movement_mechanics: Stand with balls of feet on the edge of a step. Heels hang off the edge.
Lower heels slowly below step level for a deep calf stretch.
Drive up onto toes as high as possible, squeezing calf muscles hard.
Hold top position for 1 full second before lowering again.
Repeat in a smooth, continuous rhythm without bouncing.
audio_coaching_cues: Rise as high as possible. Reach for the mountain peak.
Lower slowly. The stretch at the bottom is where growth happens.
Keep knees straight but not locked.

target_muscle_group: Quadriceps, Gluteus Maximus, Hamstrings, Core, Lower Back
cultural_lineage_roots: Rooted in the universal grounding posture found across West African farming and East Asian martial arts.
movement_mechanics: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width. Toes out about 15 degrees.
Clasp hands at chest level or extend arms forward for balance.
Initiate the squat by pushing hips back and down.
Descend until thighs are at least parallel to the ground.
Drive through entire foot to stand. Squeeze glutes hard at the top.
audio_coaching_cues: Push hips back first, then bend the knees.
Keep chest lifted throughout. Do not round your back.
Press the ground away from you as you stand up.
